summary: Strong vaporwave aesthetic with genuine craft in the game engine and visual layering, undermined by code architecture issues (1700-line god component, massive duplication), accessibility gaps, and missing basic UX states.

p0.title: Accessibility: Canvas game has no keyboard focus management
p0.evidence: SpaceInvaders.tsx: tab-index on canvas but no focus ring, no ARIA live regions, no screen-reader announcements for score changes or game state transitions.

p0.title: 1700-line god component with 622-line inline game loop useEffect
p0.evidence: SpaceInvaders.tsx is a single monolithic component. The game loop alone is 622 lines inside a useEffect callback. This prevents any reuse, testing, or maintenance.

p0.title: 60+ instances of hardcoded colors instead of design tokens
p0.evidence: Colors like '#FF71CE', '#01CDFE', '#05FFA1', '#0b0114' are hardcoded throughout the canvas rendering code instead of using the CSS custom properties already defined in index.css.

p0.title: 18 near-identical drawShape invader methods (massive duplication)
p0.evidence: drawShape0 through drawShape17 are ~35 lines each, totaling ~630 lines of nearly identical pixel-art drawing code differing only in color and shape offsets.

p1.title: text-white/45 fails WCAG AA for normal-size text (4.29:1 vs 4.5:1)
p1.evidence: Used for metric labels in CABINET STATUS card and keyboard hints. At 9px font size this is not large text, so 4.5:1 is required.

p1.title: Audio progress bar is non-interactive
p1.evidence: Progress bar is a pure visual indicator (motion.div with width %). Users cannot click or drag to seek. No <input type=range> or click handler on the track.

p1.title: No meta description, no OG tags, no favicon
p1.evidence: index.html has <title> but no <meta name=description>, no Open Graph tags, no <link rel=icon>. Lighthouse SEO will flag all of these.

p1.title: Audio autoplay may be blocked with no recovery UX
p1.evidence: When isPlaying is true and currentTrackIndex changes, the useEffect calls .play() which browsers commonly block. Error state shows text but play state stays true momentarily.

p1.title: No loading/empty state for audio tracks
p1.evidence: External MP3 URLs from soundhelix.com. No loading spinner, no network error recovery, no retry mechanism. If URLs are unreachable the player shows nothing until the error message appears.

p2.title: Header brand block has no skip-to-content link
p2.evidence: Keyboard users tab through the header logo area before reaching the game canvas. No skip link to bypass decorative header content.

p2.title: Left sidebar hidden below xl breakpoint loses mission instructions
p2.evidence: MISSION LOG with gameplay instructions is hidden at xl: breakpoint via 'hidden xl:flex'. Smaller screens lose the how-to-play context entirely.

p2.title: Game canvas renders at fixed 800x600 regardless of container size
p2.evidence: Canvas width/height attributes are hardcoded to BASE_WIDTH=800, BASE_HEIGHT=600. CSS scales it visually but the internal resolution doesn't adapt, causing blurriness on HiDPI displays.

p2.title: Simulated metrics have no semantic meaning
p2.evidence: CPU LOAD, MEMORY FREE, CORE TEMP are purely decorative random numbers. While on-brand for the aesthetic, they add visual noise without information value.

p2.title: No prefers-reduced-motion handling for game canvas animations
p2.evidence: CSS has @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) for UI animations, but the canvas-based game loop ignores this preference entirely.

strengths: Genuine craft in the vaporwave visual system: CRT vignette, scanlines, vaporwave perspective grid, neon glow tokens. The aesthetic is committed and cohesive, not surface-level.
The SpaceInvaders game engine is impressively feature-rich: 18 pixel-art invader shapes, boss battles, power-ups, particle systems, Web Audio synth SFX, UFO waves, and score persistence.
The cassette deck audio player is a standout visual metaphor with animated tape reels, TYPE II label detail, and equalizer bars that respond to play state.
risks: The 1700-line monolith makes any future feature addition high-risk for regressions.
Hardcoded hex colors in canvas code mean a brand refresh would require editing 60+ locations across the game engine.
No automated tests exist. The game logic (collision, power-ups, boss patterns) is entirely untested.
questions: Should the game state machine be extracted into a custom hook or class to enable testing and reuse?
Would a sprite-sheet approach (single image, source-rect lookup) replace the 18 drawShape methods more maintainably?
Should the canvas rendering read CSS custom properties for colors to unify the design token system?
recommendation: Start with P0-3 and P0-4 (extract color constants and consolidate drawShape methods). These are the highest-leverage refactors: they reduce the file by ~700 lines and connect the canvas code to the design system. Then tackle P0-2 by extracting the game engine into a testable module.
